verses.commentary: Therefore, I urge you, brothers and sisters, in view of God's mercy, to offer your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and pleasing to God, this is your true and proper worship. Therefore connects doctrine (chapters 1-11) to practice (chapters 12-16). In view of God's mercy motivates ethics. Living sacrifice contrasts with dead animals. We offer ourselves, alive and ongoing. Holy and pleasing describes acceptable worship. True and proper worship (logikē latreia, λογικὴ λατρεία, spiritual/reasonable service) is not ritual but self-offering.

Do not conform to the pattern of this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind. Then you will be able to test and approve what God's will is, his good, pleasing and perfect will. Negative: do not conform (syschēmatizō, συσχηματίζω, be molded by). Positive: be transformed (metamorphoō, μεταμορφόω, undergo metamorphosis). Transformation comes through mind renewal. The renewed mind discerns God's will: good, pleasing, and perfect. Christian living flows from transformed thinking.

For by the grace given me I say to every one of you: Do not think of yourself more highly than you ought, but rather think of yourself with sober judgment, in accordance with the faith God has distributed to each of you. Humility begins with accurate self-assessment. Do not think more highly (hyperphroneō, ὑπερφρονέω) warns against pride. Sober judgment (sōphroneō, σωφρονέω) means sound thinking. Faith is distributed by God in measure. This prevents boasting; all is grace.

For just as each of us has one body with many members, and these members do not all have the same function. The body metaphor illustrates unity and diversity. One body, many members. Each member has a distinct function. Diversity is by design.

So in Christ we, though many, form one body, and each member belongs to all the others. Application: the church is one body in Christ. Though many, we are united. Each member belongs to all the others (mutual belonging). This creates interdependence. No member is independent.

We have different gifts, according to the grace given to each of us. If your gift is prophesying, then prophesy in accordance with your faith. Gifts (charismata, χαρίσματα) are grace-enabled abilities. They differ according to God's distribution. Prophesying (prophēteia, προφητεία) is declaring God's word. Exercise it in accordance with faith (the faith, or the measure of faith). Gifts must align with sound doctrine.

If it is serving, then serve; if it is teaching, then teach. Serving (diakonia, διακονία) is practical ministry. Teaching (didaskalia, διδασκαλία) is instruction. Each gift should be exercised in its proper sphere. Use what you have been given.

If it is to encourage, then give encouragement; if it is giving, then give generously; if it is to lead, do it diligently; if it is to show mercy, do it cheerfully. Encouraging (parakaleō, παρακαλέω) is exhortation/comfort. Giving (metadidōmi, μεταδίδωμι) should be generous (haplotēs, ἁπλότης, simplicity, sincerity). Leading (proistēmi, προΐστημι) requires diligence (spoudē, σπουδή, earnestness). Showing mercy (eleōn, ἐλεῶν) should be cheerful (hilarotēs, ἱλαρότης). Manner matters as much as action.

Love must be sincere. Hate what is evil; cling to what is good. Sincere love (anypokritos, ἀνυπόκριτος) is without hypocrisy. Love is both affection and moral conviction. Hate evil (apostygeō, ἀποστυγέω, abhor). Cling to good (kollaomai, κολλάομαι, glue yourself to). Love discerns between good and evil.

Be devoted to one another in love. Honor one another above yourselves. Devoted (philostorgos, φιλόστοργος) suggests family affection. Honor (timē, τιμή) means esteem, value. Prefer others above yourself. This is counter-cultural, anti-pride.

Never be lacking in zeal, but keep your spiritual fervor, serving the Lord. Zeal (spoudē, σπουδή) is earnest effort. Spiritual fervor (pneumati zeō, πνεύματι ζέω, boiling in spirit) is passionate devotion. Serve the Lord (kyriō douleuō, κυρίῳ δουλεύω, slave for the Lord). Christianity is not passive but active and fervent.

Be joyful in hope, patient in affliction, faithful in prayer. Three commands: joyful hope (rejoice in confident expectation), patient affliction (endure suffering without complaint), faithful prayer (persevere in intercession). These sustain Christian life through trials.

Share with the Lord's people who are in need. Practice hospitality. Share (koinōneō, κοινωνέω) means participate, contribute. The Lord's people in need are fellow believers. Practice hospitality (philoxenia, φιλοξενία, love of strangers). Care for the body extends beyond preferences.

Bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse. A radical command echoing Jesus (Matthew 5:44). Bless (eulogeō, εὐλογέω, speak well of) persecutors. Do not curse (kataraomai, καταράομαι, invoke evil on). This is supernatural love.

Rejoice with those who rejoice; mourn with those who mourn. Empathy in joy and sorrow. Rejoice with rejoicers (share joy, not envy). Mourn with mourners (share grief, not indifference). This is emotional solidarity.

Live in harmony with one another. Do not be proud, but be willing to associate with people of low position. Do not be conceited. Harmony (to auto phroneō, τὸ αὐτὸ φρονέω, think the same) is unity of mind. Not proud (hypsēla, ὑψηλά, high things). Associate with the lowly (tapeinois, ταπεινοῖς). Not conceited (phronimoi, φρόνιμοι, wise in your own eyes). Humility overcomes class and status.

Do not repay anyone evil for evil. Be careful to do what is right in the eyes of everyone. No retaliation. Evil for evil is forbidden. Be careful (pronoeomai, προνοέομαι, think ahead) to do what is right publicly. Christian conduct should be above reproach before all.

If it is possible, as far as it depends on you, live at peace with everyone. Peace is the goal. If possible recognizes it is not always achievable. As far as it depends on you limits responsibility to what you control. We pursue peace but cannot force it.

Do not take revenge, my dear friends, but leave room for God's wrath, for it is written: It is mine to avenge; I will repay, says the Lord (Deuteronomy 32:35). Vengeance belongs to God, not us. Leave room (dote topon, δότε τόπον, give place) means step aside and let God judge. Trust God's justice rather than taking matters into your own hands.

On the contrary: If your enemy is hungry, feed him; if he is thirsty, give him something to drink. In doing this, you will heap burning coals on his head (Proverbs 25:21-22). Overcome evil with good. Feeding enemies is radical love. Burning coals may refer to shame leading to repentance, or unexpected kindness that melts hostility. Either way, love defeats hatred.

Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good. The summary principle: do not let evil master you; conquer evil with good. This is the way of the cross. Evil is defeated not by more evil but by good. Christian love transforms enemies.
